title: Stripping and staining floorboards
subtitle: Sand, stain, and finish bare timber floorboards
excerpt: How to strip old paint or varnish from timber floorboards, apply a wood stain, and finish with a hard-wearing topcoat: board-by-board preparation, sanding strategy, applying stain evenly across grain direction, and choosing between oil, wax, and lacquer finishes.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

recipeTools.notes: For lifting thick paint build-up before sanding.

glossaryTerms.definition: A penetrating or semi-transparent colouring agent for timber that colours the wood without forming a thick surface film. Penetrating stains soak into the grain; surface stains sit on the wood and are more opaque. For floorboards, an oil-based penetrating stain gives the most even result and is compatible with hardwax oil topcoats.

A topcoat for timber floors combining natural oils and waxes, formulated to penetrate and harden in the wood rather than sitting as a surface film. More durable than a straight oil, easier to repair locally than a lacquer, and more natural in appearance than a polyurethane varnish.

content.text: Stained floorboards are a practical finish for a bedroom or living room with sound timber in good condition. The process requires thorough preparation: stain applied to a floor with old varnish still in the grain will not penetrate evenly, leaving patches and tide marks. A hired floor sander is the correct tool for a full room; a detail orbital sander reaches the board edges and corners.
Fix any loose or squeaking boards. Punch all nail heads below the surface. Fill nail holes and cracks with a flexible wood filler or a mixture of fine sawdust and PVA. Sweep thoroughly.

Hire a drum or belt floor sander for the main floor area and an edge sander for the borders. Sand with the grain direction, working through grits from 40 or 60 (to strip) through 80, 100, and finally 120. Always vacuum between grits. The floor is ready when the wood is uniform in colour across its full width and depth, with no patchy areas.

along the grain direction with a brush or lint-free cloth, working one or two boards at a time. Keep a wet edge across the full width of the boards to avoid tide marks. Wipe off the surplus with a clean cloth after 5 minutes. Do not allow the stain to pool in the gaps between boards. Allow to dry for the time on the tin before the topcoat (typically 3 to 6 hours for oil-based stain).

or a compatible floor lacquer along the grain with a wide brush or short-pile applicator roller. Two coats are standard, with a light denibbing (gentle scuff with a 240-grit sanding pad) between coats once the first coat is fully dry. Allow full cure of 3 to 5 days before moving furniture back.

attrs.symptom: Stain patchy or uneven across the floor
attrs.cause: Residual varnish or old finish in the grain preventing even absorption; or stain applied without maintaining a wet edge.
attrs.fix: Sand back to bare timber and re-apply. Work faster to maintain the wet edge; apply with a cloth rather than a brush for more control.

attrs.symptom: Topcoat peeling at the edges and corners
attrs.cause: Edge sander left these areas at a coarser grit than the main floor, or old finish remained at the edges.
attrs.fix: Sand the affected areas back to bare timber with a detail sander, blend with 120 grit, and re-apply the stain and topcoat to the entire floor for an even finish.
